verses.text: If thou shalt hearken diligently unto the voice of the LORD thy God, to observe and to do all His commandments — the LORD thy God will set thee on high above all nations of the earth. Blessed shalt thou be in the city, in the field. Blessed shall be the fruit of thy body, the fruit of thy ground, the fruit of thy cattle, the increase of thy kine and the flocks of thy sheep. Blessed shall be thy basket and thy store. Blessed shalt thou be when thou comest in, when thou goest out. Guidance: Six 'blesseds' covering every room of life — city and field, body and barn, basket and store, every doorway. God's blessing is not vague; it touches the things you actually count. Hearken, and life itself becomes a series of small yeses.

Hearken, and life itself becomes a series of small yeses.

The LORD shall cause thine enemies to be smitten: come out one way, flee seven ways. He shall command the blessing upon thy storehouses; establish thee an holy people. All people of the earth shall see thou art called by the name of the LORD; they shall be afraid of thee. The LORD shall open His good treasure, the heaven, to give thee rain in season, bless all the work of thine hand. He shall make thee the head, and not the tail. Guidance: Enemies scatter seven ways; storehouses fill; rain comes on time; nations look at you and read God's name. Head, not tail — Israel's role among the peoples was always to be the messenger of God's blessing, not the recipient of someone else's.

If thou wilt not hearken unto the voice of the LORD thy God — all these curses shall come upon thee, overtake thee. Cursed shalt thou be in the city, cursed in the field. Cursed shall be thy basket and thy store. Cursed shall be the fruit of thy body, fruit of thy land, the increase of thy kine and the flocks of thy sheep. Cursed shalt thou be when thou comest in, when thou goest out. Guidance: The same six places that were blessed are now cursed — line by line, room by room. God's covenant doesn't punish by random misfortune; it withdraws the very blessings it gave. The mirror gets flipped, and the reflection is harrowing.

The LORD shall send upon thee cursing, vexation, rebuke. He shall smite thee with consumption, fever, inflammation, extreme burning, sword, blasting, mildew. He shall smite thee with the botch of Egypt, the emerods, the scab, the itch, whereof thou canst not be healed. He shall smite thee with madness, blindness, astonishment of heart. Thou shalt grope at noonday, as the blind gropeth in darkness. Guidance: The diseases of Egypt come BACK — the very plagues God used to set them free become curses they bear. Madness, blindness, astonishment — the mind goes too. The chapter is brutal not for shock value but because covenant breakage has real weight. God will not let His people drift unmolested into ruin.

Thou shalt betroth a wife, another shall lie with her: build a house, not dwell therein: plant a vineyard, not gather grapes. Thy sons and daughters shall be given unto another people. The LORD shall bring thee, and thy king, unto a nation thou hast not known; thou shalt serve other gods, wood and stone. Thou shalt become an astonishment, a proverb, a byword, among all nations. Guidance: Every long-term investment lost mid-stream. The wife, the house, the vineyard, the children — all begun, none enjoyed. And the worst: become a cautionary tale among the nations. The people who were to be a witness become a warning. The Jewish exile would later read this chapter and weep at how exactly it had landed.

Thou shalt carry much seed out into the field, gather but little in; the locust shall consume it. Thou shalt plant vineyards, not drink the wine; the worms shall eat. Thou shalt have olive trees, but not anoint thyself; thine olive shall cast his fruit. Sons and daughters shalt thou beget, they shall go into captivity. The stranger shall get up above thee very high; thou shalt come down very low. He shall lend to thee, thou shalt not lend to him: he shall be the head, thou the tail. Guidance: The agricultural blessings reversed line by line — seed, vineyard, olive. The most painful inversion: head becomes tail. Israel was meant to lend to the nations; now they borrow. The covenant in reverse is debt, locust, and an empty wine cup.

Thou shalt carry much seed out into the field, gather but little in; the locust shall consume it. Thou shalt plant vineyards, not drink the wine; the worms shall eat. Thou shalt have olive trees, but not anoint thyself; thine olive shall cast his fruit. Sons and daughters shalt thou beget, they shall go into captivity. The stranger shall get up above thee very high; thou shalt come down very low. He shall lend to thee, thou shalt not lend to him: he shall be the head, thou the tail. Guidance: The agricultural blessings reversed line by line — seed, vineyard, olive. The most painful inversion: head becomes tail. Israel was meant to lend to the nations; now they borrow. The covenant in reverse is debt, locust, and an empty wine cup.

Because thou servedst not the LORD with joyfulness in abundance — thou shalt serve thine enemies in hunger, thirst, nakedness. He shall put a yoke of iron upon thy neck. The LORD shall bring a nation against thee from far, swift as the eagle; tongue thou shalt not understand; fierce countenance, no regard for the old, no favour to the young. He shall besiege thy gates until thy walls come down. Thou shalt eat the fruit of thine own body, the flesh of thy sons and daughters, in the siege. Guidance: God names ahead what siege does to people: cannibalism. The same Israelite who served God with gratitude in the orchard now eats her own child in the alley. The text is unsparing because the writer can read history. Joyless worship invites a yoke; refused gratitude opens the gate to invasion.

If thou wilt not observe all this law — the LORD will make thy plagues wonderful, sicknesses of long continuance. Ye shall be left few in number, whereas ye were as the stars of heaven. As the LORD rejoiced to do you good, so the LORD will rejoice over you to destroy. He shall scatter thee from one end of the earth to the other; no rest for the sole of thy foot. He shall give thee a trembling heart, failing eyes, sorrow of mind. He shall bring thee into Egypt again with ships; there ye shall be sold for bondmen, and no man shall buy you. Guidance: A staggering reversal. From stars to a handful; from rescued to re-sold; from a land of promise to a planet of no rest. The last verse is the cruelest: back to Egypt, and no buyer this time. The chapter is the gravest warning in the Pentateuch — and ends on the awful quiet of unsold slaves. Hope comes in the next chapter.
